enrichment.short_description: Stoat (formerly Revolt) is a free, open-source chat and community platform built as a privacy-first alternative to Discord, Slack, and Telegram.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stoat is a free, open-source chat and community platform built as a privacy-first alternative to Discord. See features, pricing, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Stoat is a free, open-source instant messaging and community platform originally launched as Revolt on January 27, 2021, and rebranded to Stoat in October 2025 after a trademark dispute over the Revolt name. It is developed by Revolt Platforms Ltd, a company registered in London, United Kingdom. Stoat positions itself as a privacy-first alternative to Discord, Slack, and Telegram, offering servers, channels, text chat, voice chat, file sharing, and markdown support, with a Rust-based backend and a codebase released under the GNU Affero General Public License v3. There is no paid subscription tier for the core product: Stoat is entirely free to use, funded by user donations, and can be self-hosted by anyone who wants full control over their own infrastructure. The platform grew from an early Hacker News launch to over 100,000 registered users by mid-2023 and surged past 500,000 accounts in October 2024 after Discord faced regional restrictions in Turkey and Russia, with another growth wave in early 2026 following Discord age-verification changes. Clients are available for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and the web, with iOS in development. Stoat is best suited for privacy-conscious communities, open-source enthusiasts, and gaming or hobbyist groups seeking a Discord alternative without corporate data collection.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Stoat
paragraphs: Stoat is a free, open-source chat and community platform designed for people who want the group-chat experience of Discord or Slack without a surveillance-based business model behind it. The project began life as Revolt in early 2021 and was rebranded to Stoat in October 2025 after the development team received a cease-and-desist letter over the Revolt trademark.

The platform is built and maintained by Revolt Platforms Ltd, a UK-registered company, alongside an active open-source community on GitHub. Because the code is public and licensed under AGPLv3, anyone can inspect it, self-host their own instance, or contribute new features rather than relying solely on the hosted stoat.chat service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Stoat offers servers and channels for organizing communities, real-time text chat, voice chat, direct messaging, file and media sharing, and markdown formatting. It is built in Europe and designed around EU data protection principles, with a privacy policy short enough for users to actually read.

Because the backend is written in Rust and the project supports self-hosting, technical teams and privacy-focused communities can run their own independent Stoat servers instead of depending on the company's hosted infrastructure. Native and web clients are available across desktop and mobile, with iOS support in active development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Stoat has no paid subscription plan for its core chat features. The entire platform is free to use, and ongoing development is funded through user donations rather than advertising or data monetization. Communities and organizations that prefer not to manage their own servers can use the hosted stoat.chat service at no cost, while technically inclined users can self-host the open-source codebase for complete control over their data.

features.title: Servers and channels
features.description: Organize communities into servers with multiple text and voice channels, similar to Discord's structure.

features.title: Voice chat
features.description: Real-time voice communication within community servers, refreshed as part of the 2025 Stoat rebrand.

features.title: Self-hosting support
features.description: Anyone can deploy their own instance of Stoat using the open-source, Rust-based codebase.

features.title: Cross-platform clients
features.description: Native apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android, plus a web client, with iOS in development.

features.title: Privacy-first design
features.description: Built in Europe under EU data protection principles with a short, readable privacy policy and no ad-driven data mining.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Publicly available source code under the AGPLv3 license, enabling community audits and contributions.

features.title: Markdown and file sharing
features.description: Rich text formatting and file or media attachments within conversations.

features.title: Donation-based funding model
features.description: No subscription tiers gate core functionality; development is supported by voluntary user donations.

pros: Completely free with no paywalled core features
Open-source and self-hostable for full data control
Privacy-focused, EU-based data handling
Actively growing user base and rapid feature development
Familiar Discord-like interface lowers the learning curve
cons: Smaller ecosystem of bots, integrations, and third-party apps compared to Discord
iOS app was still in development as of the 2025-2026 rebrand
Rapid user growth has occasionally strained platform infrastructure
Recent rebrand from Revolt to Stoat may cause naming confusion
Fewer enterprise-oriented support or compliance options than commercial community platforms

faqs.question: Is Stoat the same product as Revolt?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stoat is the rebranded name for the chat platform previously known as Revolt. The team renamed it in October 2025 after receiving a cease-and-desist letter regarding the Revolt trademark.

faqs.question: Is Stoat free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stoat is entirely free. There is no paid subscription tier for core features, and development is funded through user donations.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Stoat?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stoat is open source under the AGPLv3 license and can be self-hosted on your own infrastructure for full control over your data.

faqs.question: What platforms does Stoat support?
faqs.answer: Stoat has native clients for Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android, plus a web client. An iOS app is in active development.

faqs.question: Who makes Stoat?
faqs.answer: Stoat is developed by Revolt Platforms Ltd, a company registered in London, United Kingdom, together with an open-source community of contributors.

faqs.question: How is Stoat different from Discord?
faqs.answer: Stoat offers similar servers, channels, and voice chat functionality, but is open source, self-hostable, ad-free, and built around EU-style data privacy principles rather than a data-driven advertising model.

faqs.question: Does Stoat support voice chat?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stoat includes a voice chat system that was refreshed as part of the October 2025 rebrand.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious online communities
use_cases.description: Groups that want Discord-style servers and channels without ad tracking or data mining can run their community on Stoat.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team or hobbyist chat
use_cases.description: Technical teams can deploy their own Stoat instance to keep all chat data on infrastructure they control.

use_cases.title: Discord alternative during regional restrictions
use_cases.description: Users in regions where Discord faces access restrictions have adopted Stoat as a functioning alternative for voice and text communication.

features: Self-hosting option with full source code access

enrichment.short_description: Stonly is an interactive guide and self-serve customer support platform that helps companies build step-by-step decision trees, walkthroughs, and AI-assisted help content.
enrichment.seo_title: Stonly Pricing and Features: Interactive Guide Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stonly builds interactive guides and AI-assisted self-service support. Compare Stonly pricing plans, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Stonly is a customer support and product adoption platform, founded in 2018 and headquartered in Levallois-Perret, France, near Paris, with roughly 51-200 employees. It lets support, product, and success teams build interactive guides, decision trees, walkthroughs, and knowledge base content that can be embedded on websites, in-app, or inside help desk tools like Zendesk, Freshdesk, and Front. Stonly also offers AI-driven automations that route customers to the right guide or answer without needing a live agent, positioning it as a self-service deflection tool that reduces support ticket volume. Pricing starts with a free Basic tier that includes 400 monthly guide views, 5 published guides, and 1 team member. Paid tiers include Starter, priced around 99 to 124 US dollars per month depending on billing cycle, which unlocks up to 30 guides, a custom knowledge base, analytics, and help desk integrations, and Business, priced around 199 to 249 US dollars per month, which adds unlimited guides, multiple knowledge bases, multilingual support, and SEO optimization. An Enterprise tier offers custom integrations, single sign-on, and dedicated support at custom pricing. Stonly is best suited for SaaS companies and e-commerce brands that want to reduce support ticket volume through guided self-service.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Stonly
paragraphs: Stonly is an interactive guide platform used by support, product, and customer success teams to build step-by-step walkthroughs, decision trees, and AI-driven help content. Instead of static help center articles, Stonly guides adapt based on the choices a user makes, walking them toward a resolution or the right next action.

Founded in 2018 and based in Levallois-Perret, France, Stonly serves companies that want to reduce inbound support ticket volume by letting customers solve problems themselves, while still giving support agents the same interactive guides to use during live conversations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Stonly's core building block is the interactive guide: a branching, clickable flow that can include text, images, video, and embedded forms. Guides can be triggered automatically based on user behavior, page context, or support tool integrations, and can be published to a help center, embedded widget, or in-product tooltip.

The platform includes analytics on guide performance and deflection rates, multilingual content support, custom branding and CSS controls, and integrations with help desks such as Zendesk, Freshdesk, and Front, as well as data tools like Segment. Newer AI capabilities help route users to the right guide automatically and can generate draft answers from existing knowledge base content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Stonly offers a free Basic tier with 400 guide views per month, 5 published guides, and 1 team member, suitable for testing the product on a small scale. The Starter plan is priced around 99 US dollars per month billed annually, or 124 US dollars per month billed monthly, and adds up to 30 guides, a custom knowledge base, analytics, and help desk integrations.

The Business plan is priced around 199 US dollars per month billed annually, or 249 US dollars per month billed monthly, and unlocks unlimited guides, multiple knowledge bases, multilingual support, and SEO optimization. Enterprise pricing is custom and adds single sign-on, private knowledge bases, white-label options, and a dedicated customer success manager.

features.title: Interactive guides and decision trees
features.description: Branching, clickable walkthroughs that adapt to user choices instead of static help articles.

features.title: AI-assisted routing
features.description: Automatically directs customers to the most relevant guide or answer based on their query.

features.title: Knowledge base builder
features.description: Create and host a searchable, brandable knowledge base alongside interactive content.

features.title: Help desk integrations
features.description: Connects with Zendesk, Freshdesk, Front, and other support tools to surface guides during live conversations.

features.title: Analytics and deflection reporting
features.description: Tracks guide views, completion rates, and support ticket deflection to measure self-service impact.

features.title: Multilingual and white-label support
features.description: Publish guides in multiple languages with custom branding on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Embeddable widgets and in-app tooltips
features.description: Deploy guides on websites, inside products, or as standalone help center pages.

features.title: User targeting and triggers
features.description: Show specific guides to specific user segments based on behavior or account attributes.

pros: Reduces support ticket volume through effective self-service guides
Flexible deployment as embedded widgets, tooltips, or a full knowledge base
Strong help desk integrations with Zendesk, Freshdesk, and Front
Detailed analytics on guide performance and deflection
Free tier available for small-scale testing
cons: Higher-tier plans can be expensive for smaller teams
Guide view limits on lower plans can be restrictive for high-traffic sites
Multilingual and white-label features are gated behind paid tiers
Building complex decision trees requires upfront content design effort
Enterprise features like SSO require custom sales conversations

faqs.question: What is Stonly used for?
faqs.answer: Stonly is used to build interactive guides, decision trees, and self-service help content that reduce customer support ticket volume and improve product onboarding.

faqs.question: Does Stonly have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stonly offers a free Basic tier with 400 guide views per month, 5 published guides, and 1 team member.

faqs.question: How much does Stonly cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start around 99 to 124 US dollars per month for the Starter tier, depending on billing cycle, with the Business tier priced around 199 to 249 US dollars per month. Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Does Stonly integrate with help desks?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stonly integrates with Zendesk, Freshdesk, Front, and other support platforms so agents can use guides directly inside live conversations.

faqs.question: Can Stonly guides be multilingual?
faqs.answer: Yes, multilingual guide support is available starting on the Starter and Business plans.

faqs.question: Who founded Stonly and where is it based?
faqs.answer: Stonly was founded in 2018 and is headquartered in Levallois-Perret, France, near Paris.

faqs.question: Does Stonly use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stonly includes AI-assisted features that help route customers to the correct guide or generate draft answers from existing content.

use_cases.title: Support ticket deflection
use_cases.description: Customer support teams embed interactive guides on help center pages so customers can resolve common issues without contacting an agent.

use_cases.title: Product onboarding walkthroughs
use_cases.description: Product teams use Stonly guides as in-app tooltips to walk new users through setup and key features.

use_cases.title: Agent-assisted troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Support agents follow Stonly decision trees during live chat or phone conversations to standardize troubleshooting steps.

features: Single language, up to 100 steps per guide

enrichment.short_description: Stoplight is an API design, documentation, and mocking platform that helps teams build APIs using a design-first workflow, now operating as part of SmartBear.
enrichment.seo_title: Stoplight: API Design and Documentation Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stoplight helps teams design, document, and mock APIs with a design-first workflow. See Stoplight pricing, features, and how it fits into SmartBear.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Stoplight is an API design, documentation, and mock-server platform founded in 2015 by Marc MacLeod in Austin, Texas. It promotes a design-first approach to building APIs, allowing teams to visually design OpenAPI specifications, generate interactive documentation, create reusable style guides and components, and spin up mock servers before writing backend code. Before being acquired, Stoplight raised roughly 21.9 to 26 million US dollars in funding, including an 11 million dollar Series B led by Next Coast Ventures in 2021. In August 2023, SmartBear announced an agreement to acquire Stoplight to broaden its API development and documentation portfolio, and Stoplight's governance, mocking, and visual editing capabilities have since been integrated into SmartBear's broader API platform. Stoplight offers a free plan for a single user and project, with paid plans historically including Basic at roughly 44 to 56 US dollars per month for 3 users, Startup at roughly 113 to 147 US dollars per month for 8 users, and Pro Team at roughly 362 to 453 US dollars per month for 15 users, plus a custom Enterprise tier. Notable customers have included Calendly, Schneider Electric, Fiserv, and PagerDuty. Stoplight is best suited for API platform teams, developer experience teams, and organizations standardizing API governance across many services.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Stoplight
paragraphs: Stoplight is a platform for designing, documenting, and mocking APIs using a design-first methodology. Rather than writing code first and documenting it afterward, teams use Stoplight to define an API's contract with OpenAPI specifications first, then build implementation against that agreed-upon design.

Founded in 2015 in Austin, Texas by Marc MacLeod, Stoplight grew into a platform used by API platform teams, developer experience teams, and enterprises standardizing API governance. In August 2023, SmartBear announced its acquisition of Stoplight, combining Stoplight's design-first tooling with SmartBear's existing API testing, documentation, and portal products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Stoplight includes a visual OpenAPI editor, interactive API documentation, and instant mock servers that let frontend and backend teams work in parallel from an agreed API contract. Reusable style guides, shared components, and models help large organizations enforce consistency across many APIs.

For larger teams, Stoplight adds multi-branch support, custom domains, activity logs, and single sign-on through LDAP or SAML, along with shared style guides and component libraries that scale governance across up to 20 teams on higher plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Stoplight offers a free plan limited to 1 user and 1 project with core design and documentation features. The Basic plan is priced around 44 US dollars per month billed annually or 56 US dollars per month billed monthly, and includes 3 users with additional users available at roughly 11 US dollars per month each on annual billing.

The Startup plan is priced around 113 US dollars per month annually or 147 US dollars monthly for 8 users, and adds private projects, multi-branch support, and custom domains. The Pro Team plan is priced around 362 US dollars per month annually or 453 US dollars monthly for 15 users, adding SSO, shared style guides, and component libraries. Enterprise pricing is custom, with unlimited projects and teams. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial.

features.title: Visual OpenAPI designer
features.description: Design API specifications visually without hand-writing raw OpenAPI YAML or JSON.

features.title: Interactive documentation
features.description: Automatically generated, browsable API documentation kept in sync with the design.

features.title: Instant mock servers
features.description: Spin up mock APIs from a specification so frontend and backend teams can work in parallel.

features.title: Reusable style guides and components
features.description: Shared models and style rules enforce consistency across many APIs and teams.

features.title: Multi-branch and version support
features.description: Manage API design changes across branches before merging into a stable specification.

features.title: Enterprise governance controls
features.description: SSO via LDAP or SAML, activity logs, and custom domains on higher-tier plans.

features.title: SmartBear platform integration
features.description: Stoplight's design and mocking tools are integrated into SmartBear's broader API development suite.

features.title: API portfolio visibility
features.description: Centralized view of an organization's full API catalog for program and platform leaders.

pros: Strong design-first workflow with visual OpenAPI editing
Mock servers accelerate parallel frontend and backend development
Reusable style guides help enforce API governance at scale
Backed by SmartBear's broader API tooling and resources post-acquisition
Free tier available for individual users testing the product
cons: Pricing scales quickly with user count on team plans
Some standalone Stoplight capabilities are being folded into SmartBear's unified platform, which can mean workflow changes for existing users
Advanced governance features like SSO require higher-tier plans
Smaller company footprint (11-50 employees) compared to larger API tooling competitors
Enterprise pricing requires a custom sales conversation

faqs.question: What is Stoplight used for?
faqs.answer: Stoplight is used to design, document, and mock APIs using a design-first workflow, letting teams agree on an API contract before writing implementation code.

faqs.question: Is Stoplight still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No. Stoplight was acquired by SmartBear in 2023, and its design, mocking, and governance capabilities are being integrated into SmartBear's broader API development platform.

faqs.question: Does Stoplight have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stoplight offers a free plan for 1 user and 1 project with core design and documentation features.

faqs.question: How much does Stoplight cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans range from roughly 44 US dollars per month for the Basic tier to roughly 362 US dollars per month for the Pro Team tier, depending on billing cycle and user count, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Who founded Stoplight?
faqs.answer: Stoplight was founded in 2015 by Marc MacLeod in Austin, Texas.

faqs.question: Does Stoplight support mock servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stoplight can generate instant mock servers directly from an OpenAPI specification.

faqs.question: What companies use Stoplight?
faqs.answer: Publicly referenced Stoplight customers include Calendly, Schneider Electric, Fiserv, and PagerDuty.

use_cases.title: Design-first API development
use_cases.description: Engineering teams define an OpenAPI contract in Stoplight before writing backend code, keeping frontend and backend work in sync.

use_cases.title: API governance at scale
use_cases.description: Large organizations use shared style guides and components to enforce consistent standards across dozens or hundreds of APIs.

use_cases.title: Interactive API documentation
use_cases.description: Teams publish auto-generated, always up-to-date documentation for internal or external API consumers.
use_cases.audience: Developer experience and technical writing teams

enrichment.short_description: Stormkit is a self-hosted deployment and hosting platform for web applications, positioned as an alternative to Vercel and Netlify with full infrastructure control.
enrichment.seo_title: Stormkit: Self-Hosted Vercel and Netlify Alternative
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stormkit is a self-hosted web app deployment platform. Compare Stormkit pricing, features, and how it works as a Vercel and Netlify alternative.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Stormkit is a deployment and hosting platform for modern web applications that positions itself as a self-hosted alternative to Vercel and Netlify. It supports Git-based deployments from GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket, with automatic builds triggered on every push, zero-downtime rolling deployments, custom domains with automatic TLS certificate provisioning, and support for frameworks and languages including Node.js, Astro, Nuxt, Next.js, Svelte, FastAPI, Go, and Rust. Because it can be self-hosted on a team's own infrastructure, Stormkit is aimed at engineering teams that want CI/CD and preview deployments without vendor lock-in or the recurring costs of fully managed platforms, with the company claiming up to 47 percent lower infrastructure costs versus comparable managed hosting. The platform reports serving over 600 million requests with 99.9 percent uptime over a 365-day period. Pricing includes a free plan with unlimited usage and community support, a Premium plan at 20 US dollars per month per seat adding analytics, audit logs, approval mode, and team features, and an Ultimate plan at 100 US dollars per month per seat adding priority support through Slack, Discord, or Teams. An Enterprise offering called Stormkit Services provides custom infrastructure setup, monitoring, and dedicated support. Specific founding year, headquarters, and employee count are not publicly confirmed.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Stormkit
paragraphs: Stormkit is a web application deployment and hosting platform built as a self-hosted alternative to services like Vercel and Netlify. It gives development teams Git-based CI/CD, preview deployments, and production hosting while allowing the underlying infrastructure to remain under the team's own control rather than a third-party cloud.

The platform is designed for teams that want the developer experience of a modern hosting platform, such as automatic deployments and zero-downtime releases, without being locked into a single vendor's infrastructure or pricing model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Stormkit automatically deploys every push to a configured branch with zero required configuration, and supports zero-downtime rolling updates so releases do not interrupt live traffic. Custom domains can be connected with automatic TLS certificate provisioning, and the platform supports a wide range of frameworks and languages including JavaScript frontends and backends, Astro, Nuxt, Next.js, Svelte, FastAPI, Go, and Rust.

Additional capabilities include real-time analytics and error monitoring, secure environment variable management for both client and server code, audit logs, and automated end-to-end testing on enterprise tiers. Because Stormkit is self-hostable, teams retain full data export capability and avoid platform lock-in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Stormkit's Free plan is 0 US dollars per month with unlimited usage and community support, requiring no credit card to start. The Premium plan is 20 US dollars per month per seat and adds analytics, audit logs, approval mode, and team collaboration features. The Ultimate plan is 100 US dollars per month per seat and adds priority support through Slack, Discord, or Microsoft Teams.

For larger organizations, Stormkit offers a custom Stormkit Services engagement, reported around 500 US dollars per month, covering infrastructure setup, service updates, monitoring, feature development, enterprise features, and direct Slack support.

features.title: Git-based automatic deployments
features.description: Every push to a configured branch triggers an automatic build and deployment with zero required configuration.

features.title: Zero-downtime rolling deployments
features.description: Releases roll out without interrupting live traffic on production applications.

features.title: Custom domains with automatic TLS
features.description: Connect custom domains and automatically provision TLS certificates.

features.title: Multi-framework and multi-language support
features.description: Supports Node.js, Astro, Nuxt, Next.js, Svelte, FastAPI, Go, and Rust applications.

features.title: Self-hosting option
features.description: Deploy Stormkit on your own infrastructure for full control and no vendor lock-in.

features.title: Analytics and error monitoring
features.description: Real-time performance tracking and error monitoring built into the platform.

features.title: Secure environment variables
features.description: Manage configuration secrets for both client-side and server-side applications.

features.title: Audit logs and approval mode
features.description: Team governance controls available on the Premium and Ultimate plans.

pros: Self-hosting option avoids vendor lock-in common with managed platforms
Free plan offers unlimited usage without requiring a credit card
Broad framework and language support beyond just JavaScript frontends
Reports significantly lower infrastructure costs versus managed alternatives
Zero-downtime deployments and automatic TLS reduce operational overhead
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community compared to Vercel or Netlify
Founding year, headquarters, and team size are not publicly disclosed
Self-hosting requires more setup and maintenance effort than a fully managed platform
Per-seat pricing on paid tiers can add up for larger teams
Enterprise support engagement pricing is not fully transparent

faqs.question: What is Stormkit used for?
faqs.answer: Stormkit is used to deploy, host, and manage web applications with Git-based CI/CD, either on Stormkit's hosted service or self-hosted on a team's own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Is Stormkit free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stormkit offers a free plan with unlimited usage and community support, with no credit card required to get started.

faqs.question: How is Stormkit different from Vercel or Netlify?
faqs.answer: Stormkit offers a self-hosted deployment option so teams can run the platform on their own infrastructure, aiming to avoid vendor lock-in and reduce hosting costs compared to fully managed platforms.

faqs.question: What frameworks does Stormkit support?
faqs.answer: Stormkit supports JavaScript frontend and backend frameworks such as Next.js, Nuxt, Astro, and Svelte, as well as Go, Rust, and FastAPI applications.

faqs.question: How much does Stormkit cost?
faqs.answer: Stormkit has a free tier, a Premium plan at 20 US dollars per month per seat, and an Ultimate plan at 100 US dollars per month per seat, plus a custom enterprise services engagement.

faqs.question: Does Stormkit support custom domains?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stormkit supports custom domains with automatic TLS certificate provisioning.

faqs.question: Can Stormkit be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stormkit can be self-hosted on a team's own infrastructure for full control over deployments and data.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted CI/CD for web apps
use_cases.description: Engineering teams deploy Stormkit on their own infrastructure to get Git-based CI/CD without depending on a third-party cloud vendor.

use_cases.title: Cost-conscious hosting for growing products
use_cases.description: Startups and agencies use Stormkit to reduce hosting costs versus fully managed platforms while keeping a similar deployment experience.

use_cases.title: Multi-framework application hosting
use_cases.description: Teams running a mix of JavaScript, Go, and Rust services use Stormkit to deploy all of them from a single platform.

features: Priority support via Slack, Discord, or Microsoft Teams

enrichment.short_description: Storyblok is a headless content management system with a visual editor that lets marketers and developers manage structured content across any frontend or channel.
enrichment.seo_title: Storyblok: Headless CMS with Visual Editor Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Storyblok is a headless CMS with a visual editor for marketers and developers. Compare Storyblok pricing plans, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Storyblok is a headless content management system founded in 2017 and headquartered in Linz, Austria, with several hundred employees spread across multiple continents. It combines an API-first, component-based content architecture with a visual editor that lets non-technical marketers preview and edit content in context, while developers build and reuse structured content components across websites, apps, and other channels. Storyblok raised 47 million US dollars in a 2022 funding round aimed at growing its headless CMS for non-technical users, and by that time its tools were used by roughly 74,000 companies. Pricing starts with a free Starter plan for 1 space and 1 user seat with 100GB of monthly traffic and 100,000 API requests. The Growth plan is 99 US dollars per month, or roughly 90.75 US dollars per month billed annually, and includes 5 user seats, 400GB of traffic, and 1 million API requests. The Growth Plus plan is 349 US dollars per month, or roughly 319.91 US dollars per month annually, and includes 15 seats, 1TB of traffic, and 4 million API requests, plus a 45-day free trial. Enterprise-grade Premium and Elite plans offer custom pricing with GraphQL, SSO, SCIM, custom roles and workflows, and uptime SLAs up to 99.99 percent. Storyblok is best suited for marketing teams, agencies, and enterprises that need a flexible, component-driven CMS across multiple digital channels.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Storyblok
paragraphs: Storyblok is a headless content management system that separates content storage and delivery from the frontend presentation layer. Content is delivered through APIs to any website, mobile app, or digital channel, while a visual editor lets marketers and content teams see and edit changes in a real-time preview rather than working blind in a raw content form.

Founded in 2017 in Linz, Austria, Storyblok has grown into a widely adopted headless CMS, raising 47 million US dollars in funding in 2022 to expand its platform for both technical and non-technical users. The company reports its tools have been used by tens of thousands of companies across industries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Storyblok's component-based architecture, sometimes called bloks, lets developers build reusable content blocks that marketers can then assemble into pages without needing a developer for every change. The visual editor shows a live, in-context preview of content as it will appear on the live site, bridging the usual gap between headless CMS flexibility and traditional CMS ease of use.

The platform supports REST and GraphQL APIs, multi-language localization, role-based access, and a workflow automation layer called FlowMotion for teams that need approval chains or content publishing automations. Enterprise plans add single sign-on, SCIM provisioning, custom roles and workflows, and high-availability uptime SLAs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Storyblok's Starter plan is free, with 1 space, 1 user seat, 100GB of monthly traffic, 100,000 API requests, 2 locales, and 25,000 AI credits. The Growth plan costs 99 US dollars per month, or about 90.75 US dollars per month billed annually, and includes 5 user seats, 400GB of traffic, 1 million API requests, and 100,000 AI credits, with additional locales available at 20 US dollars each.

The Growth Plus plan costs 349 US dollars per month, or about 319.91 US dollars per month billed annually, and includes 15 seats, 1TB of traffic, 4 million API requests, 10 locales, and 200,000 AI credits, plus a 45-day free trial. Premium and Elite Enterprise plans use fully custom, consumption-based pricing and add GraphQL, SSO, SCIM, custom roles and workflows, and uptime SLAs between 99.9 and 99.99 percent.

features.title: Visual, in-context editor
features.description: Marketers can preview and edit content exactly as it will appear on the live site rather than editing in a raw form.

features.title: Component-based content model (bloks)
features.description: Developers build reusable content components that non-technical editors can assemble into pages.

features.title: REST and GraphQL APIs
features.description: Content is delivered via API to any website, app, or digital channel.

features.title: Multi-language localization
features.description: Manage content across multiple locales, with additional locales available on paid plans.

features.title: FlowMotion workflow automation
features.description: Automate content approval chains and publishing workflows with configurable execution tiers.

features.title: Enterprise governance
features.description: SSO, SCIM provisioning, custom roles, and workflows available on Premium and Elite plans.

features.title: AI credits and tooling
features.description: Built-in AI credits included on every plan to support AI-assisted content workflows.

features.title: High availability infrastructure
features.description: Enterprise plans offer uptime SLAs up to 99.99 percent for mission-critical content delivery.

pros: Visual editor bridges the usability gap common in headless CMS platforms
Component-based model promotes strong content reuse across channels
Free Starter tier available for small projects
Flexible API delivery via REST or GraphQL to any frontend
Well-funded company with enterprise-grade governance features
cons: Costs scale quickly once traffic, API requests, or seats exceed the base tiers
Additional locales beyond the included amount cost extra on Growth plans
Enterprise features like SSO and SCIM require a custom-priced Premium or Elite plan
Learning the component-based (bloks) content model takes onboarding time for new teams
AI credit limits can be consumed quickly on content-heavy sites

faqs.question: What is Storyblok used for?
faqs.answer: Storyblok is used as a headless CMS to manage structured content and deliver it via API to websites, mobile apps, and other digital channels, while giving marketers a visual, in-context editing experience.

faqs.question: Does Storyblok have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan is free and includes 1 space, 1 user seat, 100GB of traffic, and 100,000 API requests per month.

faqs.question: How much does Storyblok cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 99 US dollars per month for Growth and 349 US dollars per month for Growth Plus, with custom pricing for Enterprise-grade Premium and Elite plans.

faqs.question: Where is Storyblok headquartered?
faqs.answer: Storyblok is headquartered in Linz, Austria, and was founded in 2017.

faqs.question: What does headless CMS mean in Storyblok's context?
faqs.answer: It means content is stored and managed independently from how it is displayed, and delivered via REST or GraphQL API to any frontend, app, or channel.

faqs.question: Does Storyblok support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, Storyblok supports multi-locale content management, with additional locales beyond the included amount available for an extra fee on some plans.

faqs.question: Is Storyblok suitable for non-technical marketers?
faqs.answer: Yes, its visual, in-context editor is designed so marketers can edit and preview content without needing a developer for routine changes.

use_cases.title: Multi-channel content delivery
use_cases.description: Enterprises use Storyblok to manage content once and deliver it consistently across websites, mobile apps, and other digital touchpoints.

use_cases.title: Marketing-led website management
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use the visual editor to update landing pages and campaign content without waiting on developer support.

use_cases.title: Agency and developer projects
use_cases.description: Digital agencies build reusable, component-based content structures in Storyblok for multiple client websites.

enrichment.short_description: Storybook is a free, open-source frontend workshop for building, testing, and documenting UI components in isolation, maintained by the team behind Chromatic.
enrichment.seo_title: Storybook: Open-Source UI Component Development Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Storybook is a free, open-source tool for building and testing UI components in isolation. See its features, framework support, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Storybook is a free, open-source frontend workshop for building, documenting, and testing UI components in isolation from a full application. It began as React Storybook, released in April 2016 by Kadira, a bootstrapped Sri Lankan startup led by Arunoda Susiripala that was known for contributions to the Meteor community. After Kadira shut down in December 2016, the project went idle before being handed to the open-source community in 2017, and it is now maintained primarily by the team behind Chromatic, a visual testing product built by former Storybook maintainers. Storybook renders individual components with specific props and mock data inside an isolated iframe, using a declarative format called Component Story Format based on ES6 modules, so components can be developed and reviewed without booting an entire application. It integrates with testing tools such as Jest, Playwright, and Cypress for interaction, accessibility, and visual testing, and supports a broad range of frameworks including React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, and even React Native, Android, iOS, and Flutter. Storybook itself is entirely free and open source, hosted on GitHub with tens of thousands of stars, and used by hundreds of companies and thousands of developers. Paid options exist only for adjacent, separately maintained products like Chromatic's visual testing service, not for Storybook itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Storybook
paragraphs: Storybook is an open-source development tool that lets frontend engineers build and test UI components in isolation, outside the context of a full running application. It started as React Storybook, first released in April 2016 by Kadira, a Sri Lankan startup previously known for its contributions to the Meteor ecosystem.

After Kadira closed at the end of 2016, Storybook development stalled until the open-source community took over maintenance in 2017. Today the project is maintained largely by the team behind Chromatic, a visual testing platform built by former Storybook maintainers, though Storybook itself remains fully open source and free.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Storybook lets developers write stories, which are declarative descriptions of a component's different states and variations, and render them inside an isolated iframe so styling and behavior can be reviewed without interference from application-level logic. Stories follow the open Component Story Format standard, making them reusable by external testing tools.

The tool supports interaction testing, accessibility checks, and visual testing add-ons, and maintains an interactive index of every component and its states for team reference. Framework support extends well beyond the web, covering React, Vue, Angular, and Svelte, as well as React Native, Android, iOS, and Flutter for mobile component development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Storybook is completely free and open source, with no paid tier for the core tool itself. It is distributed on GitHub, where it has attracted more than 90,000 stars, and is used by hundreds of companies and thousands of individual developers. Optional, separately maintained products such as Chromatic offer paid visual testing and review services that integrate with Storybook, but Storybook's own component-development functionality carries no license cost.

features.title: Component isolation
features.description: Build and preview UI components inside an isolated iframe, separate from full application logic and context.

features.title: Component Story Format
features.description: An open, ES6-module-based standard for describing component variations, reusable by external testing tools.

features.title: Interactive component index
features.description: A browsable catalog of every UI component and its documented states for team reference.

features.title: Testing integrations
features.description: Works with Jest, Playwright, and Cypress for interaction, accessibility, and visual testing workflows.

features.title: Broad framework support
features.description: Supports React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, and mobile frameworks including React Native, Android, iOS, and Flutter.

features.title: Add-on ecosystem
features.description: Extensible via a large community add-on library for accessibility checks, design tokens, and more.

features.title: Living documentation
features.description: Stories double as up-to-date, interactive documentation for a design system or component library.

features.title: Open-source and free
features.description: Fully free to use with an active community and no licensing cost for core functionality.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Large, active community and extensive add-on ecosystem
Broad framework support across web and mobile
Stories double as living documentation for design systems
Integrates cleanly with popular testing tools like Jest, Playwright, and Cypress
cons: Requires developer effort to write and maintain stories for every component
Not a hosted product, so teams manage their own deployment and CI setup
Some advanced workflows like automated visual review require a separate paid product such as Chromatic
Initial setup and configuration can be non-trivial for complex projects
No official commercial support channel for the open-source core

faqs.question: What is Storybook used for?
faqs.answer: Storybook is used to build, test, and document UI components in isolation from a full application, so developers can review each component's states independently.

faqs.question: Is Storybook free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Storybook is completely free and open source with no paid tier for its core functionality.

faqs.question: Who maintains Storybook?
faqs.answer: Storybook is maintained primarily by the team behind Chromatic, building on a project originally created by Kadira and later handed to the open-source community.

faqs.question: What frameworks does Storybook support?
faqs.answer: Storybook supports React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, and other web frameworks, as well as mobile frameworks including React Native, Android, iOS, and Flutter.

faqs.question: Is Storybook the same as Chromatic?
faqs.answer: No. Storybook is a free, open-source component development tool, while Chromatic is a separate, paid visual testing and review product made by former Storybook maintainers that integrates with Storybook.

faqs.question: Does Storybook support testing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Storybook integrates with tools like Jest, Playwright, and Cypress for interaction, accessibility, and visual testing.

faqs.question: When was Storybook created?
faqs.answer: Storybook began as React Storybook, first released in April 2016 by the Kadira team.

use_cases.title: Design system development
use_cases.description: Design system teams use Storybook to build, document, and review reusable UI components in isolation before they ship to production apps.
use_cases.audience: Frontend engineers and design system teams

use_cases.title: Component-driven testing
use_cases.description: Teams write interaction and accessibility tests against components in Storybook, catching regressions before integration.

use_cases.title: Living UI documentation
use_cases.description: Organizations use Storybook as an always-current, interactive catalog of every component and its variations for cross-team reference.
use_cases.audience: Product and design teams collaborating with engineering

features: Full component isolation and story authoring
Testing integrations with Jest, Playwright, and Cypress

enrichment.short_description: Strapi is an open-source, self-hostable headless CMS that gives developers full control over content APIs, with an optional managed Strapi Cloud offering.
enrichment.seo_title: Strapi: Open-Source Headless CMS Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Strapi is an open-source, self-hostable headless CMS with REST and GraphQL APIs. Compare Strapi pricing, Strapi Cloud plans, and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Strapi is an open-source, JavaScript-based headless content management system founded in 2016 and headquartered in Paris, France, with a remote-first team of roughly 51 to 200 employees across more than 10 countries. It gives developers a self-hostable, MIT-licensed CMS with REST and GraphQL APIs, customizable content types, and role-based access control, making it a common choice for teams that want full ownership of their content infrastructure rather than a fully managed SaaS CMS. Strapi has raised roughly 45 to 49 million US dollars in funding, including a 31 million dollar Series B round led by CRV in 2022, and reports more than 450 enterprise customers. Strapi's self-hosted Community plan is free forever under an MIT license with unlimited entries and API calls. The paid, self-hosted Growth plan is 45 US dollars per month for 3 seats, with additional seats at 15 US dollars per month each, adding Strapi AI credits, live preview, and content history. Enterprise self-hosted pricing is custom and adds review workflows, audit logs, and SOC 2 reporting. Strapi also offers a managed Strapi Cloud service with Starter at 35 US dollars per month per project, Pro at 90 US dollars per month per project, and Business at 450 US dollars per month per project, plus a custom Business+ tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Strapi
paragraphs: Strapi is an open-source headless content management system built with JavaScript and Node.js, designed to give developers full control over content modeling, APIs, and hosting. Unlike fully managed SaaS CMS platforms, Strapi's core is self-hostable and released under the MIT license, so teams can run it on their own infrastructure at no licensing cost.

Founded in 2016 and headquartered in Paris, France, Strapi has grown into one of the most widely adopted open-source CMS projects, backed by tens of millions of dollars in venture funding and used by more than 450 enterprise customers alongside a large open-source community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Strapi provides customizable content types, role-based access control, and both REST and GraphQL APIs out of the box, with unlimited content entries, API calls, locales, and components across all plans, including the free Community tier. A plugin system lets developers extend the admin panel and API behavior for custom workflows.

Paid self-hosted plans add Strapi AI content assistance, live preview of content changes, scheduled releases, and extended content history, while Enterprise tiers add review workflows, audit logs, and SOC 2 compliance reporting. For teams that prefer not to manage infrastructure, Strapi Cloud offers fully managed hosting with built-in email, a global CDN, custom domains, and automated backups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Strapi's self-hosted Community plan is free forever under the MIT license, with unlimited entries, API calls, content types, locales, and components. The self-hosted Growth plan costs 45 US dollars per month and includes 3 seats, with additional seats at 15 US dollars per month each, adding Strapi AI credits, live preview, scheduled releases, and 30 days of content history. Self-hosted Enterprise pricing is custom and adds review workflows, audit logs, 365 days of content history, and SOC 2 reporting.

For managed hosting, Strapi Cloud's Starter plan is 35 US dollars per month per project with 100,000 API requests and 50GB of storage and bandwidth. The Pro plan is 90 US dollars per month per project with 1 million API requests and 250GB of storage and bandwidth. The Business plan is 450 US dollars per month per project with 10 million API requests, 1,000GB of storage and bandwidth, and a 99.9 percent uptime SLA, with a custom Business+ tier above that. Annual billing on Strapi Cloud saves up to 17 percent.

features.title: Self-hostable, open-source core
features.description: MIT-licensed CMS that can run on any infrastructure with no licensing fee for the Community edition.

features.title: REST and GraphQL APIs
features.description: Content is exposed automatically through both REST and GraphQL endpoints.

features.title: Customizable content types
features.description: Developers define content structures and relationships specific to their application.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Granular permissions for admin users and API consumers.

features.title: Strapi AI content assistance
features.description: AI-assisted content creation available on paid Growth and Enterprise plans, billed by credits.

features.title: Strapi Cloud managed hosting
features.description: Fully managed deployment option with global CDN, automated backups, and custom domains.

features.title: Plugin and extension system
features.description: Extend the admin panel and API behavior with custom or community-built plugins.

features.title: Enterprise governance
features.description: Review workflows, audit logs, and SOC 2 reporting available on Enterprise plans.

pros: Free, open-source Community edition with unlimited entries and API calls
Full self-hosting control avoids vendor lock-in on core content infrastructure
Both REST and GraphQL APIs supported natively
Strong enterprise adoption with 450-plus enterprise customers and significant funding
Optional Strapi Cloud removes the need to manage infrastructure directly
cons: Self-hosting requires ongoing server management, security patching, and scaling effort
Strapi AI credits are limited on paid plans, with overage charged per additional credit
SSO is an additional paid add-on rather than included in the Growth plan
Strapi Cloud project-based pricing can add up for organizations running many separate projects
Advanced compliance features like SOC 2 reporting require the custom Enterprise tier

faqs.question: Is Strapi free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Strapi's self-hosted Community edition is free forever under the MIT license, with unlimited entries, API calls, content types, and locales.

faqs.question: What is the difference between self-hosted Strapi and Strapi Cloud?
faqs.answer: Self-hosted Strapi runs on infrastructure you manage yourself, while Strapi Cloud is a fully managed hosting service with project-based pricing that handles infrastructure, backups, and scaling for you.

faqs.question: How much does Strapi Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Strapi Cloud starts at 35 US dollars per month per project for the Starter plan, with Pro at 90 US dollars per month and Business at 450 US dollars per month, plus a custom Business+ tier.

faqs.question: Does Strapi support GraphQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, Strapi automatically exposes content through both REST and GraphQL APIs.

faqs.question: Who founded Strapi and where is it based?
faqs.answer: Strapi was founded in 2016 and is headquartered in Paris, France, with a remote-first team spread across more than 10 countries.

faqs.question: Does Strapi include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, Strapi AI content assistance is available on the paid Growth and Enterprise self-hosted plans, billed through monthly credit allowances.

faqs.question: Is Strapi suitable for enterprise use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Strapi reports more than 450 enterprise customers and offers an Enterprise tier with review workflows, audit logs, and SOC 2 compliance reporting.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted content backend
use_cases.description: Development teams deploy Strapi on their own infrastructure to power websites and apps with full data ownership.

use_cases.title: Omnichannel content delivery
use_cases.description: Organizations use Strapi's REST and GraphQL APIs to deliver the same content to web, mobile, and IoT applications.

use_cases.title: Managed CMS via Strapi Cloud
use_cases.description: Teams that want Strapi's flexibility without managing servers use Strapi Cloud for hosting, backups, and scaling.

enrichment.short_description: Streak is a CRM that runs entirely inside Gmail, letting sales, support, and other teams manage pipelines and contacts without leaving their inbox.
enrichment.seo_title: Streak: CRM for Gmail Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Streak is a CRM built inside Gmail for sales and support teams. Compare Streak pricing plans, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Streak is a customer relationship management tool built directly inside Gmail, founded in 2011 by former Google product manager Aleem Mawani and co-founder Omar Ismail. The company went through Y Combinator's Summer 2011 batch and is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with a small team of roughly 11 to 50 employees. Rather than requiring users to switch to a separate CRM application, Streak overlays pipeline management, shared team visibility, mail merge, email tracking, and automation directly onto the Gmail interface, making it popular with sales, recruiting, support, and deal-flow teams that live in their inbox. Streak offers a free plan with 500 contacts and 50 mail merge emails per day. The Solo plan is 15 US dollars per user per month with 5,000 contacts and 800 mail merge emails per day. The Pro plan is 49 US dollars per user per month and includes unlimited contacts, shared pipelines, and 20 AI credits per user per month. The Pro+ plan is 69 US dollars per user per month and adds advanced reports, integrations, automations, and 150 AI credits per user per month. The Enterprise plan is 129 US dollars per user per month, requires a minimum of 10 users, and adds custom roles, data validation, and dedicated support. Annual billing offers a 20 percent discount across paid plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Streak
paragraphs: Streak is a CRM that lives entirely inside Gmail rather than as a separate application. It was founded in 2011 by Aleem Mawani, a former Google product manager, and Omar Ismail, and went through Y Combinator's Summer 2011 accelerator batch before graduating in early 2012.

The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and built Streak to solve its own founders' sales workflow needs before realizing teams across recruiting, support, deal flow, and project management wanted the same inbox-native approach to tracking relationships and pipelines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Streak adds pipeline views directly into the Gmail interface, letting teams track deals, candidates, support tickets, or any other process as it moves through customizable stages without leaving their inbox. Shared pipelines give teammates visibility into the same data, while email tracking shows when recipients open messages.

The platform includes mail merge for sending personalized bulk emails, reusable email snippets, and a thread splitter for managing multiple conversations in one thread. Higher-tier plans add advanced reporting, third-party integrations and automations, custom roles, data validation rules, and AI credits for AI-assisted workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Streak's Free plan includes 500 contacts and 50 mail merge emails per day, along with core features like email tracking, snippets, and thread splitting. The Solo plan is 15 US dollars per user per month and raises limits to 5,000 contacts and 800 mail merge emails per day, aimed at individual users.

The Pro plan is 49 US dollars per user per month and includes unlimited contacts, shared pipelines, and 20 AI credits per user per month. The Pro+ plan is 69 US dollars per user per month, adding advanced reports, integrations, automations, and 150 AI credits per user per month, with a 14-day free trial. The Enterprise plan is 129 US dollars per user per month, requires at least 10 users, and adds custom roles, data validation, and dedicated support including a direct line to Streak's CEO. Annual billing offers roughly a 20 percent discount versus monthly billing across paid tiers.

features.title: Pipeline management inside Gmail
features.description: Track deals, candidates, or any process through customizable stages without leaving the inbox.

features.title: Shared team pipelines
features.description: Give teammates visibility into the same contacts, deals, and email threads.

features.title: Email tracking
features.description: See when recipients open tracked emails sent from Gmail.

features.title: Mail merge
features.description: Send personalized bulk emails directly from Gmail with daily sending limits by plan.

features.title: Reusable email snippets
features.description: Save and reuse common email templates and responses.

features.title: AI credits
features.description: AI-assisted features available on paid plans, with credit allowances scaling by tier.

features.title: Integrations and automations
features.description: Connect Streak to other tools and automate repetitive CRM tasks on Pro+ and above.

features.title: Custom roles and data validation
features.description: Enterprise-tier controls for managing user permissions and data quality.

pros: Works entirely inside Gmail, requiring no context switching to a separate app
Free plan available for individuals with light contact volume
Flexible pipeline system adaptable to sales, recruiting, or support workflows
Shared pipelines give teams visibility without a heavyweight CRM rollout
Backed by Y Combinator with over a decade of operating history
cons: Tightly coupled to Gmail, so it is not useful for teams on other email platforms
Unlimited contacts only start at the Pro tier and above
Enterprise plan requires a minimum of 10 users
Advanced reporting and automation are gated behind Pro+ and higher plans
Small company size (11-50 employees) relative to larger CRM competitors

faqs.question: What is Streak used for?
faqs.answer: Streak is used as a CRM inside Gmail, letting sales, recruiting, support, and other teams track pipelines, contacts, and deals without leaving their inbox.

faqs.question: Is Streak free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Streak offers a free plan with 500 contacts and 50 mail merge emails per day, along with email tracking and snippets.

faqs.question: How much does Streak cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans range from 15 US dollars per user per month for Solo to 129 US dollars per user per month for Enterprise, with a roughly 20 percent discount for annual billing.

faqs.question: Who founded Streak?
faqs.answer: Streak was founded in 2011 by Aleem Mawani and Omar Ismail, and went through Y Combinator's Summer 2011 batch.

faqs.question: Does Streak work outside of Gmail?
faqs.answer: Streak is built specifically as a Gmail extension and CRM layer, so it is designed to work inside the Gmail interface rather than as a standalone application.

faqs.question: Does Streak support mail merge?
faqs.answer: Yes, mail merge is available on all plans, with daily sending limits that increase from the Free plan through Enterprise.

faqs.question: Where is Streak based?
faqs.answer: Streak is headquartered in San Francisco, California, United States.

use_cases.title: Sales pipeline management
use_cases.description: Sales teams track deals through customizable pipeline stages directly inside Gmail, without adopting a separate CRM tool.

use_cases.title: Recruiting and candidate tracking
use_cases.description: Recruiters use Streak's pipeline views to track candidates through interview stages using their existing email workflow.

use_cases.title: Customer support and deal flow tracking
use_cases.description: Support and business development teams use shared pipelines to manage tickets or investment deal flow collaboratively.
use_cases.audience: Support teams and venture or business development teams

features: 20 AI credits per user per month
150 AI credits per user per month
500 AI credits per user per month
